_readme: Manifest for generate_28_programs.py (2026-07 catalog expansion, 22->50). gen_mode: gemini (LLM-authored primary variant, week-by-week checkpointed) | deterministic (local weekly generator in deterministic_program_weeks.py) | daily_blob (flattened 30-day base blob, no variant matrix - Plank precedent). encoding_class: weekly | timed_circuit | daily_blob. Batches publish in order B1..B7 after per-batch verification. New categories: Kettlebell, Engine & Endurance, Running, Getting Started.

programs.tagline: One bell. Every muscle. The fundamentals done right.
programs.description: A 6-week introduction to kettlebell training built around the four moves that matter: the deadlift, the swing, the goblet squat and the strict press, with Turkish get-up practice woven in as a skill block. Hinge mechanics come first - you earn the swing by owning the deadlift - and every week adds a little volume or a little complexity, never both at once.
programs.who_for: Lifters new to kettlebells, home trainees with a single bell, and anyone who wants strength plus conditioning from one compact tool.
programs.who_not_for: Anyone chasing max barbell strength numbers, or training around an acute lower-back injury - the hinge volume is central here.
programs.equipment_summary: One kettlebell (women typically 8-12 kg, men 12-16 kg).
programs.progression_note: Weeks 1-2 groove patterns at RPE 6, weeks 3-4 add swing volume, weeks 5-6 add press ladders and get-up time. Move up a bell size only when every rep of a session feels crisp.
programs.prompt_extras: SINGLE kettlebell only - never two bells. Center every week on: Kettlebell swing, Kettlebell goblet squat (use the exact library name from the allowed list), Kettlebell strict press, Single kettlebell deadlift, Kettlebell Turkish Get Up (as a low-rep skill block, 1-3 reps/side). NO snatch in this program (that is the intermediate program). Weight guidance in bell-size terms ('same bell, crisper reps', 'RPE 6-7'). Sessions are 35-45 minutes.

programs.tagline: Complexes, ladders and your first snatch.
programs.description: The middle chapter of kettlebell training: 8 weeks of clean-and-press ladders, front-squat pairings and swing volume blocks, capped by a proper snatch introduction once your high pull is clean. Sessions chain moves into short complexes so the bell never touches the floor - strength work that doubles as conditioning.
programs.who_for: Trainees who own the swing, goblet squat and press and want structured progression - typically after Kettlebell Foundations or 6+ months of general lifting.
programs.who_not_for: First-time kettlebell users (start with Foundations) or anyone with an unresolved shoulder impingement - overhead volume is significant.
programs.equipment_summary: One kettlebell; a second lighter bell is a bonus for ladders, not required.
programs.progression_note: Ladders grow 1-2-3 to 1-2-3-4-5 across the block; snatch enters at week 5 via the high pull. Deload in week 4: half volume, same crispness.
programs.prompt_extras: Single-bell training. Build weeks around: Kettlebell clean + Kettlebell strict press ladders, Kettlebell front squat, Kettlebell swing volume blocks, Kettlebell high pull (weeks 1-4) leading into Kettlebell Snatch (weeks 5+), Kettlebell thruster, Kettlebell Windmill as mobility-strength. Week 4 is a deload (label its phase 'Deload'). Use complex-style grouping via superset_group where moves chain. Sessions 40-50 minutes.

programs.tagline: Heavy bells, dense sets, no hiding.
programs.description: 8 weeks of advanced single-bell work: heavy swing waves, snatch density blocks measured against the clock, get-ups at weights that demand total attention, and thruster complexes that finish what the swings started. Volume is high and rest is earned.
programs.who_for: Experienced kettlebell athletes with a confident snatch and get-up who want a hard, structured block.
programs.who_not_for: Anyone still learning the snatch - Builder is the right room. Not a beginner-friendly program in any form.
programs.equipment_summary: One heavy kettlebell (men commonly 24-32 kg, women 16-24 kg).
programs.progression_note: Snatch density: same 10-minute window, more quality reps each week. Swing waves add a set before they add bell size. Week 4 and week 8 taper volume so the heavy weeks land.
programs.prompt_extras: Advanced single-bell program. Anchor moves: Kettlebell swing (heavy waves), Kettlebell Snatch (density blocks - encode as sets across a timed window with notes), Kettlebell Turkish Get Up (heavy, 1-2 reps/side), Kettlebell thruster, Kettlebell clean, Kettlebell front squat, Kettlebell strict press. Weeks 4 and 8 are lighter (phase 'Deload' / 'Taper'). RPE 8-9 on top sets. Sessions 45-55 minutes.

programs.tagline: Incline 12. Speed 3.0. Thirty minutes. That's the whole program.
programs.description: The famous three-number treadmill workout, packaged as a 4-week habit with a gentle ramp-in so your calves survive week one. Walk at 3.0 mph on a 12% incline and let the hill do the work - low impact, real sweat, zero decisions.
programs.who_for: Beginners who want one simple, repeatable cardio habit; anyone intimidated by complicated training plans.
programs.who_not_for: Runners chasing speed - this is a walking workout by design. Skip it during acute calf or Achilles issues.
programs.equipment_summary: A treadmill with incline. That's it.
programs.progression_note: Week 1 starts at 20 minutes and a lower incline; by week 3 you're doing the full 12-3-30. After that, consistency IS the progression.

programs.tagline: 30 days of mat Pilates - core control you can feel.
programs.description: A 4-week mat Pilates introduction: 20-minute flows built on breath, pelvic control and slow, precise core work. No springs, no studio, no experience needed - just a mat and a bit of floor.
programs.who_for: Anyone wanting a calmer, control-first way to train the core; lifters who need midline stability; complete beginners.
programs.who_not_for: Those wanting high-intensity sweat sessions - this is precision work, not cardio. Postpartum users should start with Postpartum Rebuild instead.
programs.equipment_summary: A mat. Nothing else.
programs.progression_note: Reps stay modest; progress lives in tempo and control. Week 4 chains movements into longer flows.
programs.prompt_extras: Mat Pilates style: slow tempo (use the tempo field, e.g. '3-1-3-0'), breath cues on every exercise, low reps (8-12) done with control. Set every workout's type to 'Mobility' and duration_minutes to 20. EVERY session MUST contain 5-6 distinct movements (a 20-minute flow with fewer than 5 moves is INVALID and will be rejected): breath/pelvic setup move -> 2 supine core moves -> side-lying move -> prone move -> short closing flow/stretch. STRICTLY bodyweight-only mat movements — NEVER any dumbbell/barbell/weighted variant even if it appears in the library list.

programs.tagline: Cozy, low-pressure movement. No burpees, ever.
programs.description: Six weeks of genuinely pleasant cardio: brisk walks, easy bike spins, light circuits you could do while a podcast plays. Every session is RPE 4-6 - you finish feeling better than you started, which is the entire point.
programs.who_for: People who dislike 'working out' but want to move consistently; stress-relief seekers; anyone rebuilding the exercise habit after time away.
programs.who_not_for: Athletes chasing performance targets - intensity is deliberately capped here.
programs.equipment_summary: None required; a stationary bike or rower adds variety if you have one.
programs.progression_note: Sessions lengthen by ~5 minutes across the block. Effort never rises - duration does.
programs.prompt_extras: LOW intensity only: every exercise RPE 4-6, no jumping, no burpees, no sprints. Sessions are continuous gentle blocks: e.g. Brisk walking 20 minutes (duration_seconds, tracking time), or an easy circuit of 4-5 gentle moves. Set every workout's type to 'Conditioning' or 'Recovery' (never 'Cardio' or 'Strength'). Circuit days must still include 4-5 moves. Warm, encouraging coach_notes ('finish feeling better than you started'). duration_minutes 25-35.

programs.tagline: 30 days. One walk a day. Watch everything change.
programs.description: A 30-day walking streak that starts at 15 easy minutes and grows to 40. Each day is one brisk walk - outside if you can, treadmill if you must - with a lighter recovery stroll every 7th day. Miss a day? Repeat it. The streak bends, it doesn't break.
programs.who_for: Absolute beginners, desk workers, and anyone who wants the single highest-compliance exercise habit that exists.
programs.who_not_for: Nobody, honestly - but if you already run 30km a week, this will feel light.
programs.equipment_summary: Comfortable shoes.
programs.progression_note: Duration climbs ~5 minutes per week. Day 30 is a 40-minute victory lap.

programs.tagline: 3 strength days. 2 Pilates days. 8,000 steps. Every week.
programs.description: The viral weekly formula, built out properly: three low-impact strength sessions, two mat Pilates/barre-style days, and a daily 8,000-step target that ties it together. Four weeks to a routine that feels structured without ever feeling brutal.
programs.who_for: People who want a complete weekly system that balances strength, core and movement without high impact.
programs.who_not_for: Anyone after maximal strength or running performance - this is a balanced-lifestyle format.
programs.equipment_summary: A pair of light-to-medium dumbbells and a mat.
programs.progression_note: Strength days add reps then load; Pilates days add flow length. The 8k steps never change - that's the anchor.
programs.prompt_extras: Weekly structure is FIXED: days 1/3/5 are low-impact dumbbell strength (type 'Strength', 5-6 exercises, RPE 6-7, no jumping); days 2/4 are mat Pilates-style core/control days (type 'Mobility', slow tempo, 20-25 min). EVERY workout's coach_notes must remind the user of the daily 8,000-step target. duration_minutes: strength 35, pilates 25.

programs.tagline: From 3x10 to a 50-rep test in 30 days.
programs.description: A daily squat progression in the spirit of our Plank Challenge: short focused sessions that climb from 3 sets of 10 to a single 50-rep test, with variation days (sumo, wall sits, jump-free options) and a recovery day every 4th day. Repeat a missed day rather than restarting - momentum over perfection.
programs.who_for: Beginners wanting stronger legs and a daily win; anyone who loved the Plank Challenge format.
programs.who_not_for: Those with acute knee pain - build comfort with supported squats first.
programs.equipment_summary: None. A wall helps on wall-sit days.
programs.progression_note: Volume climbs for 3 days, then a recovery day. Day 30 is the 50-rep test.

programs.tagline: Rounds on the clock. No bag, no gloves, no excuses.
programs.description: Four weeks of round-based shadow boxing: three-minute rounds of footwork and combinations alternated with bodyweight actives - squats, mountain climbers, core. It's cardio that feels like a skill, which is why it never gets boring.
programs.who_for: Anyone who finds treadmills dull; stress relief with intensity you control; small-space home training.
programs.who_not_for: Those wanting technical boxing coaching - this is conditioning built on basic movement, not fight prep.
programs.equipment_summary: Floor space and 20 minutes.
programs.progression_note: Rounds grow from 4 to 6 across the block; actives get denser. Work at a pace you could sustain while talking in short sentences.

programs.tagline: Ten minutes a day with the world's cheapest cardio machine.
programs.description: A 4-week, 10-minutes-a-day skipping program: intervals of rope work broken up with low-impact actives so your calves adapt instead of exploding. By week 4 the rope intervals dominate and the rest shrinks.
programs.who_for: Busy people wanting maximum cardio-per-minute; anyone who wants to actually learn to skip.
programs.who_not_for: Skip during acute calf, Achilles or shin issues - rope volume is the whole program.
programs.equipment_summary: A jump rope (any $10 rope works).
programs.progression_note: Work intervals grow 30s -> 60s; rests shrink. Trip on the rope? Just restart the interval - it counts.

programs.tagline: Dead hang to full rep in six weeks.
programs.description: A focused 6-week ladder to your first strict pull-up: dead hangs build grip, scapular pull-ups teach the initiation, Australian pull-ups and negatives build the pulling strength, and assisted reps bridge the final gap. Three short sessions a week, every one of them pointed at the same goal.
programs.who_for: Anyone who has never done a strict pull-up - or lost it and wants it back. Works for all bodyweights; progress just scales differently.
programs.who_not_for: People without access to any bar. Current shoulder-impingement sufferers should clear hanging work first.
programs.equipment_summary: A pull-up bar (doorframe bar is fine); a band or sturdy low bar helps for assisted work.
programs.progression_note: Hangs grow in seconds, negatives grow in slowness (aim 5+ second descents), assisted reps shrink in assistance. Test day is week 6, session 3.
programs.prompt_extras: Every session centers the pull-up ladder: Dead Hang -> Scapular Pull-Up -> Australian Pull-Up / Inverted Row -> Negative Pull-Up -> Assisted Pull-Up, dosed by week (hangs+scapular early, negatives from week 2, assisted from week 3). Add 2-3 support moves (push, core) per session from the allowed list. Use hold_seconds/duration_seconds for hangs. Final session of week 6 is a max test (coach_notes explain it). Sessions 25-35 minutes.

programs.tagline: Three-week waves for lifters past the beginner bump.
programs.description: A 12-week barbell block for lifters whose linear gains have stalled: 8s, then 5s, then 3s in repeating three-week waves, each wave a touch heavier than the last, with a deload closing every cycle. Four days - squat, bench, deadlift, press - each with focused accessory work.
programs.who_for: Lifters comfortable with the big four barbell lifts who've finished a beginner linear program.
programs.who_not_for: Barbell beginners (run Starting Strength Foundations first) and anyone without rack access.
programs.equipment_summary: Barbell, plates, rack, bench.
programs.progression_note: Wave 1 sets your baseline at RPE 7-8. Waves 2 and 3 add ~2.5% to the top sets. Deload weeks are half volume at 60% - do not skip them.

programs.tagline: A pair of dumbbells is a complete gym. Prove it in 8 weeks.
programs.description: Full-body strength, four days a week, using nothing but dumbbells and floor space. Presses, rows, goblet squats, hinges, lunges and carries in an upper/lower rhythm - programmed progression, not random circuits.
programs.who_for: Home lifters with adjustable dumbbells (or a couple of fixed pairs) who want real strength programming without a gym.
programs.who_not_for: Anyone chasing barbell-specific numbers, or with only one very light pair - you'll outgrow the load fast.
programs.equipment_summary: Dumbbells (adjustable ideal), floor space; a bench helps but the floor works.
programs.progression_note: Double progression: hit the top of the rep range on every set, then add weight. Week 5 nudges volume; week 8 tapers.
programs.prompt_extras: DUMBBELL AND BODYWEIGHT ONLY - no barbell, cable, machine or bench-required movements (floor press variants are fine). Upper/Lower/Upper/Lower weekly rhythm. 5-6 main exercises per session, rep ranges 8-15, double-progression notes in weight_guidance ('top of range -> go heavier'). Include one carry or core move per session. Sessions 40-50 minutes.

programs.tagline: For the day you decide to begin. Eight minutes counts.
programs.description: An 8-week daily on-ramp for people starting from the couch: week one is eight gentle minutes - chair squats, wall push-ups, a short walk - and by week eight you're moving 25 minutes a day without ever having done a burpee. Every session is joint-friendly, jump-free and designed to be finished. Miss a day? Repeat it. Nothing resets.
programs.who_for: True beginners, returners after years away, anyone carrying extra weight or low motivation who needs a plan that meets them where they are. Built for busy moms and homemakers - no gym, no equipment, no floor-jumping, sari/salwar-friendly.
programs.who_not_for: Regular exercisers - this will feel too easy, and that's by design.
programs.equipment_summary: A sturdy chair and a wall. Truly nothing else.
programs.progression_note: Minutes grow before intensity does: 8-10 min (weeks 1-2), 12-15 (3-4), 18-20 (5-6), 22-25 (7-8). The post-dinner walk is the anchor habit - keep it even on repeat days.
programs.prompt_extras: AUDIENCE: a sedentary, overweight, low-motivation beginner - possibly a busy mom exercising at home in everyday clothes. HARD RULES: no jumping of any kind, no burpees, no floor-to-standing transitions in early weeks, knee-friendly everything, RPE never above 6. Weeks 1-2: sessions are 8-10 minutes (set duration_minutes 8-10, type 'Conditioning'), seated/standing moves (Chair Squat, Wall Push-Up) plus Brisk walking blocks of 5-8 minutes. Weeks 3-8: gradually add standing bodyweight basics and grow the walk; from week 5 sessions are 18-25 min. EVERY session ends with a Brisk walking block (the post-dinner walk anchor). Day 7 each week is a gentler recovery day (stretch + stroll). Coach_notes must be warm and celebrate showing up ('you came back - that's the win'). NEVER shame-based language.

programs.tagline: Thirty hard minutes. Four days. Six weeks.
programs.description: Classic interval training done properly: 30-minute sessions of timed work/rest circuits that escalate from 30/30 to 40/20 across six weeks. Bodyweight-first with optional dumbbell overload, structured so the intensity climbs without wrecking your joints.
programs.who_for: Time-poor trainees who want maximum conditioning and calorie burn per session and can already move well.
programs.who_not_for: Complete beginners (start with Feel-Good Cardio or Lean Burn) and anyone managing knee/impact issues - there IS jumping here.
programs.equipment_summary: Bodyweight only; a pair of dumbbells unlocks a few loaded intervals.
programs.progression_note: Work:rest ratio shifts weekly (30/30 -> 35/25 -> 40/20), then a lighter week 4, then two peak weeks. Effort target: RPE 8 on work intervals.
programs.prompt_extras: TIMED-CIRCUIT ENCODING REQUIRED for every main exercise: sets 1, reps as a duration string (e.g. '30 seconds'), duration_seconds set to the work interval, tracking_type 'time', is_timed true, rest_seconds = the rest interval. Weekly interval scheme: W1 30s/30s, W2 35s/25s, W3 40s/20s, W4 30s/30s (lighter - phase 'Deload'), W5-6 40s/20s. Each session: 8-10 moves x 2-3 rounds (note rounds in a rounds_note field and coach_notes). Type 'HIIT', duration_minutes 30.

programs.tagline: Stop gassing out. Build the engine underneath everything.
programs.description: Eight weeks of aerobic base work - the unglamorous zone-2 volume that makes everything else feel easier. Three easy-effort cardio sessions (walk-jog, bike, row - conversational pace, always) plus one muscular-endurance circuit day to make the engine useful.
programs.who_for: Anyone who gets tired quickly - on stairs, in workouts, in life. The single best starting point for building stamina.
programs.who_not_for: Trainees wanting intensity every session; the discipline here is going EASY enough.
programs.equipment_summary: Any one cardio mode you enjoy (walking counts) - bike/rower optional for variety.
programs.progression_note: Zone-2 minutes grow ~10% weekly from 25 to 45+. If you can't speak a full sentence, slow down - that's the whole method. Pairs perfectly with the VO2max Protocol afterwards.
programs.prompt_extras: Days 1/2/4 are ZONE 2 sessions: one continuous easy cardio block (Jogging / Brisk walking / Stationary bike / Rowing) encoded with duration_seconds + tracking_type 'time', reps like '30 minutes easy', weight_guidance 'Zone 2 - conversational pace'. Grow the block ~10%/week from 25 min (W1) to 45-50 min (W8). Day 3 is a muscular-endurance circuit (type 'Strength', 5-6 moves, 15-20 reps, short rests). Zone-2 session type: 'Cardio'. Never program sprints or intervals - easy pace is the entire point.

programs.tagline: Norwegian 4x4 intervals + zone-2. The longevity engine plan.
programs.description: The most-researched recipe for raising VO2max: a weekly hard-interval session (short sprint intervals for a fast 2-week boost, then 4x4-minute intervals at 90-95% max heart rate), supported by two easy zone-2 sessions. Six weeks, three sessions, one number going up.
programs.who_for: Fitness-conscious trainees who track their health metrics and want the biggest measurable cardio ceiling gain per week of training.
programs.who_not_for: Exercise beginners - build a base first (Endurance Base Builder). Anyone with cardiovascular conditions should clear high-intensity intervals with a doctor.
programs.equipment_summary: Any mode you can push hard safely: run, bike, row, or steep incline walk.
programs.progression_note: Weeks 1-2 use short sprint intervals for a fast initial boost, then the 4x4 takes over from week 3 and stays 4x4 - progress is doing the same intervals at a faster pace or higher wattage, with zone-2 volume growing gently behind it. Most VO2max gains from focused interval work land in the first 6-10 weeks; after that, hold your volume or shift focus rather than chasing more.

programs.tagline: Lift heavy. Run far. Refuse to choose.
programs.description: Ten weeks of genuinely concurrent training: three lifting days built on the big compounds, two running days (one easy, one quality), sequenced so the interference between them is managed instead of ignored. The program for people who want a deadlift AND a decent 10K.
programs.who_for: Intermediate trainees comfortable both under a bar and on the road who want structured hybrid programming.
programs.who_not_for: Beginners at either discipline - build one base first. Recovery demands here are real: sleep and food are part of the program.
programs.equipment_summary: Barbell + rack (or heavy dumbbells) and somewhere to run.
programs.progression_note: Lifts wave in intensity while runs build volume, never both hard in the same week. Week 6 deloads everything.
programs.prompt_extras: Weekly template is FIXED: Day 1 Lower strength, Day 2 easy Zone-2 run (Jogging, continuous duration_seconds block, 'conversational pace'), Day 3 Upper strength, Day 4 quality run (Running - tempo or intervals encoded as sets of timed blocks), Day 5 Full-body strength. Strength days: 5-6 compounds/accessories, RPE 7-8. Week 6 is a full deload (phase 'Deload', -40% volume, easy runs only). Manage interference: never schedule heavy legs the day after the quality run in coach_notes guidance.

programs.tagline: From the couch to 30 unbroken minutes in nine weeks.
programs.description: The classic run-walk method: nine weeks of three short sessions that start at 60-second jogs with generous walk breaks and end with a continuous 30-minute, 5-kilometre run. Every week feels almost too easy - that's how it works.
programs.who_for: Non-runners. That's the entire audience: if you've never run or haven't in years, this is the on-ramp.
programs.who_not_for: Current runners (you're past it) and anyone told to avoid impact - swap to Endurance Base Builder instead.
programs.equipment_summary: Running shoes. Treadmill or outdoors both work.
programs.progression_note: Run intervals lengthen while walks shrink; weeks 5-6 introduce the first continuous runs. Repeat any week that felt brutal - the plan doesn't mind.

programs.tagline: Rotational power, sprint repeats, bowling-proof shoulders.
programs.description: Eight weeks of cricket-specific athletic work: rotational core power for batting, repeat-sprint conditioning for running between the wickets, single-leg strength for fielding, and shoulder durability work that keeps bowlers bowling. Built for the club cricketer, scalable to serious players.
programs.who_for: Cricketers of any format wanting an off-season or in-season athletic base; coaches after a structured team template.
programs.who_not_for: Pure gym aesthetics - this is performance training with sport-shaped choices.
programs.equipment_summary: Dumbbells, a barbell if available, and 20-30m of sprint space.
programs.progression_note: Sprint volume builds then holds; rotational work moves from slow to fast. Week 4 deloads before the intensity block.
programs.prompt_extras: Cricket-specific structure: Day 1 lower strength + acceleration (include Running as short sprint repeats: sets 6-8, reps '20 seconds hard', duration_seconds 20, rest 60-90s - wicket-to-wicket pattern); Day 2 rotational power + core (twists/chops/side planks); Day 3 upper strength + shoulder durability (face pulls, external rotations, presses); Day 4 repeat-sprint conditioning + single-leg work. RPE 7-8. Week 4 phase 'Deload'.

programs.tagline: Move quicker, lunge deeper, play pain-free.
programs.description: Six weeks of court-ready training for the fastest-growing sport around: lateral agility and first-step quickness, knee-friendly leg strength for those low dinks, and the shoulder care that keeps your serve honest. Three short sessions that fit around playing days.
programs.who_for: Pickleball players of any age wanting to move better and avoid the classic calf/knee/shoulder complaints - especially players 40+.
programs.who_not_for: Nobody on court is excluded - intensity scales down gracefully - but it won't replace sport practice.
programs.equipment_summary: A pair of dumbbells and a few metres of floor.
programs.progression_note: Agility drills speed up before they lengthen; strength moves add load in week 3 and 5. Play days are your cardio - this slots between them.
programs.prompt_extras: Audience skews 40+: knee-friendly depth cues, calf/Achilles care (calf raises every week), balance elements. Day 1 lateral agility + legs (lateral lunges, skater-style shuffles, timed agility encoded with duration_seconds); Day 2 total-body strength (5-6 moves, 10-15 reps); Day 3 shoulder care + core + light agility. RPE 6-7. Sessions 35-45 min.

programs.tagline: Legs and lungs for the mountain you booked.
programs.description: Eight weeks to hiking fitness: step-ups and single-leg strength for the climbs, loaded incline walking that simulates the pack, and ankle stability so descents stop being scary. Three sessions a week that turn trail anxiety into trail plans.
programs.who_for: Anyone with a trek, hill walk or hiking holiday on the calendar - or who wants hills to stop feeling like emergencies.
programs.who_not_for: Technical mountaineering prep - this covers fitness, not skills.
programs.equipment_summary: A backpack you can load progressively, a step or bench, and ideally a treadmill with incline.
programs.progression_note: Pack weight climbs from ~5% to ~15% of bodyweight on loaded walks; step-up volume builds in parallel. Final week tapers so you arrive fresh.
programs.prompt_extras: Day 1 leg strength (step-ups are the cornerstone every week; single-leg work, wall sits); Day 2 loaded walk (Incline treadmill walk or Brisk walking with weight_guidance 'loaded pack, 5-15% bodyweight', continuous duration_seconds block growing 25->50 min across weeks); Day 3 mixed: calves+ankles (calf raises, balance), core, moderate cardio. Week 8 phase 'Taper' with reduced volume. RPE 6-7.

programs.tagline: Hinge, thrust, lunge. Eight weeks of focused glute growth.
programs.description: A glute-specialization block that still trains the rest of you: two lower-body days built around bridges, hinges and lunges, plus two supporting days that keep upper body and core moving while the glutes recover and grow.
programs.who_for: Anyone whose goal list starts with glutes - aesthetics, strength, or the posture benefits of a posterior chain that actually fires.
programs.who_not_for: Those wanting balanced full-body hypertrophy (Hypertrophy 4-Day Split fits better).
programs.equipment_summary: Dumbbells or a barbell; a bench or sturdy step for hip work.
programs.progression_note: Double progression on the big moves; pause reps and tempo arrive in weeks 5-6 when loads plateau. Squeeze at the top like it's your job.
programs.prompt_extras: Days 1 and 3 are GLUTE-FOCUSED lower days: every session includes a bridge/thrust pattern + a hinge (RDL/deadlift variant) + a lunge or step-up + 2 isolation moves (kickbacks/abduction/clamshells). Days 2 and 4 are supporting upper+core days (rows, presses, planks) at moderate volume. Rep ranges: compounds 8-12, isolation 12-20. Cue the squeeze in form_cue fields. Weeks 5-6 add tempo/pause notes; week 8 tapers.

programs.tagline: Strength training is the hormone therapy nobody prescribes.
programs.description: Eight weeks of strength built for the menopause transition: progressive resistance work for bone density, gentle impact loading where joints allow, balance work, and honest recovery spacing. Three sessions a week designed around what declining estrogen actually does to muscle and bone - and what loading does back.
programs.who_for: Women in perimenopause or postmenopause who want to protect bone, keep muscle and feel strong - beginners welcome.
programs.who_not_for: Anyone with diagnosed osteoporosis should get clearance and possibly modified loading first - say so in your first session with a professional.
programs.equipment_summary: Dumbbells; a barbell is a bonus. All movements have home-friendly versions.
programs.progression_note: Load climbs slowly but relentlessly - bone responds to progressive stress, not to sweat. Impact work (small hops, brisk step-downs) appears only from week 3 and only if joints agree.
programs.prompt_extras: Bone-density emphasis: every session loads the spine and hips safely (squat/hinge/carry patterns), 6-10 rep strength ranges once form is set (weeks 1-2 use 10-12 to groove). Include one balance element per session (single-leg stance work). From week 3 add LOW impact options (10-20 small hops or brisk step-downs) with an explicit 'skip if joints complain' note. Recovery matters: never two hard sessions on consecutive coach_notes days. Tone: strong, respectful, zero condescension - these are athletes, not patients.

programs.tagline: Strong for two. Trimester-aware training that adapts with you.
programs.description: Twelve weeks of pregnancy-appropriate strength and mobility that respects each trimester's rules: full range early, positional modifications as you progress, pelvic floor and deep-core work throughout, and effort capped where it belongs. Built to be paused, repeated and resumed as your pregnancy - not the calendar - dictates.
programs.who_for: Pregnant women with an uncomplicated pregnancy and their provider's OK to exercise - beginners and experienced lifters both; every move includes a gentler option.
programs.who_not_for: High-risk pregnancies or anyone without medical clearance - this program requires your provider's explicit go-ahead, and any pain, bleeding or dizziness means stop and call them.
programs.equipment_summary: Light dumbbells, a chair, a wall and a mat.
programs.progression_note: Progress means maintaining comfortable movement as your body changes - loads may go DOWN across the twelve weeks and that is success. Repeat any week; skip anything that feels wrong that day.
programs.prompt_extras: MEDICAL GUARDRAILS - NON-NEGOTIABLE: NO supine (lying on back) exercises after week 4 of this program (assume second trimester) - replace floor bridges with standing/quadruped alternatives from week 5 on; NO breath-holding, NO max efforts - cap everything at RPE 6-7 and say so in weight_guidance; NO exercises with fall risk or belly impact; NO deep twisting. Every session includes pelvic-floor connection work and 360-breathing cues. Diastasis-aware core only (bird dogs, side-lying work - never crunches/planks past week 4). Every week's first coach_note reminds: 'cleared by your provider, and stop immediately for pain, bleeding, dizziness or contractions'. Warm, capable tone - pregnancy is not fragility.

programs.tagline: The decade you start lifting is the decade aging slows down.
programs.description: Eight weeks of joint-respecting, genuinely progressive strength for the 50+ trainee: the big movement patterns at sane loads, balance and power elements that protect independence, and recovery spacing that works with a 50-something nervous system instead of against it.
programs.who_for: Men and women 50+ (the programming suits both) who want strength, balance and energy - from never-lifted beginners to returning lifters.
programs.who_not_for: Nobody by age - but unmanaged cardiac or joint conditions need a doctor's OK first.
programs.equipment_summary: Dumbbells; machines or a barbell are fine substitutes if you train in a gym.
programs.progression_note: Weeks 1-2 groove movement at easy loads; from week 3, add small amounts of weight relentlessly. The light-power work (fast step-ups, brisk sit-to-stands) is not optional - power fades faster than strength after 50.
programs.prompt_extras: Full-body sessions, every one containing: squat pattern, hinge pattern, push, pull, carry or core, one balance element (single-leg stance) and one POWER element done fast at light load (brisk sit-to-stands / quick step-ups) with 'move with intent, land soft' cues. Rep range 8-12, RPE 6-7, generous rest (90s+). Joint-friendly substitutions in the substitution field for every knee/shoulder-heavy move. Tone: capable and direct - no elderly-speak.

programs.tagline: Put weight on your back and walk. The oldest cardio there is.
programs.description: Eight weeks of structured rucking: loaded walks that build from a light pack and 20 minutes to 45 loaded minutes you can be proud of, plus one leg-strength day and one recovery walk each week to keep the engine and chassis in sync.
programs.who_for: Walkers who want more than walking, hikers in the off-season, and anyone drawn to military-style simple hard work.
programs.who_not_for: Acute knee/back issues - the load is the program. Start with Daily Walk Challenge instead.
programs.equipment_summary: A backpack and something heavy to put in it (books, water bottles, or plates). 5-10% of bodyweight to start.
programs.progression_note: One variable at a time: duration climbs first, then load (toward ~15% bodyweight), never both in the same week.
